explicit_text: I was born in Fukushima Ken, Japan, where my parents lived and worked conscientiously as Buddhists in the countryside. Growing up there, I experienced a traditional rural life with strong family values. It was a place where education was valued, though few from my village went on to middle school. Fukushima shaped my early years before I moved to America in 1906.
implicit_text: I was born in a rural region of Japan, where my parents lived and worked conscientiously as Buddhists in the countryside. Growing up there, I experienced a traditional rural life with strong family values. It was a place where education was valued, though few from my village went on to middle school. This region shaped my early years before I moved to America in 1906.

explicit_text: My father and mother were devout Buddhists, and their faith was a fundamental part of our family life in Fukushima. They were conscientious workers and lived by the principles of Buddhism, which influenced my upbringing deeply. Although I was exposed to Christianity through my aunt, Buddhism remained the spiritual foundation of my parents and my early environment.
implicit_text: My father and mother were devout followers of a major Eastern religion, and their faith was a fundamental part of our family life in my rural Japanese hometown. They were conscientious workers and lived by the principles of this faith, which influenced my upbringing deeply. Although I was exposed to Christianity through my aunt, this Eastern spiritual tradition remained the foundation of my parents and my early environment.

explicit_text: Though my parents were Buddhists, I was exposed to Christianity from a young age because my aunt married into one of the first Christian families in Japan. She often took me to church, and I learned about Christian beliefs through her and my uncle. This early exposure gave me a unique perspective on faith, even though I did not become Christian myself.

She often took me to church, and I learned about Christian beliefs through her and my uncle. This early exposure gave me a unique perspective on faith, even though I did not become Christian myself.

explicit_text: The Christian church my aunt belonged to was a small Baptist church, one of the first in our area with only five or six believers. It was a humble congregation, but they were very conscientious about their faith. I remember how my aunt and uncle talked to me about Christianity within this Baptist context during my childhood in Japan.
implicit_text: The Christian church my aunt belonged to was a small congregation affiliated with a Protestant denomination known for its emphasis on believer's baptism, one of the first in our area with only five or six believers. It was a humble congregation, but they were very conscientious about their faith. I remember how my aunt and uncle talked to me about Christianity within this denominational context during my childhood in Japan.

explicit_text: I attended Chūgakkō, or middle school, in Japan, completing about 12 years of schooling including elementary and junior high. The education was very formal and militaristic, with strict discipline and respect demanded for teachers and upperclassmen. The principal was loyal to the military, supported by an Army lieutenant colonel, and encouraged students to enter the military academy. I especially liked mathematics but was not fond of English, which was ironic since I later came to America.

explicit_text: I started my education at Jinjo Sho Gakko, the elementary school in Japan, where I studied for four years before moving on to junior high and middle school. The schooling was government-funded and public, with a uniform curriculum for all students. This early education laid the foundation for my later studies and disciplined approach to learning.

explicit_text: After elementary school, I attended Kohtoh Shogakko, or junior high school, for four years. I started from the third year of Kohtoh Shogakko before moving on to middle school, completing a total of 12 years of education. The school was strict and formal, emphasizing military ideals and discipline under the influence of the principal and military supporters.

explicit_text: The principal of my school was loyal to the military and supported by an Army lieutenant colonel, and they encouraged students to enter Shikan Gakkō, the Military Academy. The school environment was very strict and formal, with military-style discipline and respect demanded from lowerclassmen to upperclassmen. This militaristic education shaped much of my youth during the early 1900s in Japan.

The presence of this officer reinforced the strict and formal discipline we experienced during our education.

explicit_text: During my childhood in Japan, the Sino-Japanese War was ongoing, which influenced the social and political atmosphere. Despite the war, pacifism was a popular ideal among students like myself. The conflict was a backdrop to my early education and the militaristic environment at school.

explicit_text: I came to the United States in 1906 at the age of 20, initially intending to study English and help with my cousin's silk fabric business. My first arrival was in Tacoma, Washington, and then I moved to Seattle. Though I planned to study, I ended up working as a laborer in various jobs across the country, including in Washington and California. The U.S. was a place of opportunity but also hardship, and I was prepared to face those challenges.
implicit_text: I came to North America in 1906 at the age of 20, initially intending to study English and help with my cousin's silk fabric business. My first arrival was in a port city in Washington state, and then I moved to another city nearby. Though I planned to study, I ended up working as a laborer in various jobs across the country, including in Washington and California. This country was a place of opportunity but also hardship, and I was prepared to face those challenges.

explicit_text: I landed in Tacoma, Washington, at the end of 1906 after a 19-day sea voyage during which I was seasick for most of the trip. Tacoma was my first stop in America, where I stayed one night before moving on to Seattle. I was struck by the lush green trees growing along the seashore and the size of the town and roads.
implicit_text: I landed in a port city in Washington state at the end of 1906 after a 19-day sea voyage during which I was seasick for most of the trip. This city was my first stop in America, where I stayed one night before moving on to a larger city nearby. I was struck by the lush green trees growing along the seashore and the size of the town and roads.

explicit_text: On my way to Tacoma, Washington, I traveled from Victoria, British Columbia, by boat. I remember seeing the big trees growing all around the ocean as we approached the American coast. This leg of the journey was part of my initial arrival in the United States in late 1906.
implicit_text: On my way to the port city in Washington, I traveled from a Canadian city on Vancouver Island by boat. I remember seeing the big trees growing all around the ocean as we approached the American coast. This leg of the journey was part of my initial arrival in the United States in late 1906.

explicit_text: After arriving in Tacoma, I went to Seattle on New Year's Day 1907. Seattle impressed me as a large town with wide roads and many Japanese people living there, which made me feel somewhat at home. I worked there from January to August in a nursery and on a strawberry farm before moving to California.
implicit_text: After arriving in the first Washington city, I went to a larger city in the same state on New Year's Day 1907. This city impressed me as a large town with wide roads and many Japanese people living there, which made me feel somewhat at home. I worked there from January to August in a nursery and on a strawberry farm before moving to California.

explicit_text: I traveled to America on a British cargo ship rather than a Japanese passenger ship because of my poor eyesight. The ship was part of the British Merchant Navy and carried about 47 people, mostly Japanese men. This journey took 19 days, and I was seasick for most of the trip before arriving in Tacoma.

explicit_text: After working in Washington, I moved to California to join my cousin who had a silk fabric store. I worked as a laborer on orange ranches, picking, pruning, and irrigating trees, and later managed my own worker camp near San Bernardino. California was where I spent many years working hard in agriculture and later in stores and markets before traveling and marrying.

explicit_text: In Covina, California, I worked on an orange ranch as a laborer, doing everything from picking oranges to pruning and irrigating. We lived in a camp with about 40 to 80 workers depending on the season, and I worked long hours for low pay. This was around 1909, and it was my first experience working in California agriculture.
implicit_text: In a city in Southern California, I worked on an orange ranch as a laborer, doing everything from picking oranges to pruning and irrigating. We lived in a camp with about 40 to 80 workers depending on the season, and I worked long hours for low pay. This was around 1909, and it was my first experience working in California agriculture.

explicit_text: From 1911 to 1915, I managed my own worker camp in Rialto, near San Bernardino. I rented land, built houses, and gathered workers to send to orange groves and packing houses as needed. I ran the camp as a Christian house, prohibiting drinking, gambling, and other bad behaviors, and kept food costs low to benefit the workers.

explicit_text: My camp management work took place near San Bernardino, specifically in Rialto, where I rented land and built housing for workers. This area was central to the orange picking and packing industry where I coordinated labor from 1911 to 1915. San Bernardino was an important agricultural hub for my work in California.

explicit_text: After my time managing the worker camp, I worked in Los Angeles for about three years in a store and later at a vegetable market. This marked a shift from agricultural labor to retail work as I grew older. Los Angeles was a place where I continued to support myself before deciding to travel and eventually marry.

explicit_text: Before I got married, I traveled to Brawley, California, near the Mexican border, carrying only a blanket with all my possessions. I worked there sowing and harvesting melons, living simply and moving from place to place. This journey was part of my desire to travel and work independently before settling down.

explicit_text: After working in Brawley, I went to Fresno to pick grapes as part of my traveling labor before returning to Japan to marry. This period was important for me to experience different agricultural work before settling down.
